name: Residential sensing intensity and night scheduling
consequences: Intensifying sensing directly increases data fidelity and reduces missed parasomnia events, enabling more reliable longitudinal phenotyping. Downstream, it heightens participant privacy exposure, staff workload, and potential discomfort with surveillance, which can affect recruitment and retention.
options: Intensify sensing by implementing full PSG on all nights for all participants, thereby maximizing data fidelity but increasing participant burden and staffing demands.
Maintain a tiered acquisition model with low-burden nightly monitoring for most nights, reserving full PSG for flagged events to balance data quality with comfort.
Introduce adaptive scheduling guided by interim event rates to optimize nights allocation, prioritizing high-information sessions while preserving workflow efficiency and privacy expectations.
review: Data richness vs participant privacy and fatigue is the core tension; the options miss explicit safeguards such as enhanced consent granularity and data minimization beyond the proposed streams.

name: Participant recruitment criteria flexibility and triage
consequences: Relaxing inclusion criteria expands potential enrollments and accelerates sample accrual, but increases heterogeneity; this can complicate downstream analyses and interpretation of longitudinal patterns. Tightening screening improves data consistency but slows enrollment and may limit generalizability.
options: Relax inclusion criteria to broaden recruitment by encompassing a wider range of NREM parasomnia presentations, accelerating enrollment but increasing phenotype heterogeneity.
Tighten screening to enroll highly defined NREM parasomnia phenotypes with stricter exclusions, improving data consistency but potentially prolonging recruitment and reducing sample size.
Introduce adaptive pre-admission remote screening to triage candidates quickly while preserving ethical safeguards and informed consent.
review: Core tension is recruitment speed versus phenotypic purity; the options overlook potential biases from rapid enrollment and do not quantify attrition risk or representativeness.

name: Event-triage development and validation strategy
consequences: Developing a semi-automated event-triage pipeline increases throughput and consistency in adjudication. However, reliance on automated flags can introduce systematic misclassification if not continuously calibrated, requiring ongoing validation across nights and participants.
options: Develop a semi-automated event-triage pipeline using multi-sensor features, validated against dual human scoring to quantify performance and inform iteration.
Rely on expert clinician adjudication for all events without automation to maximize accuracy and interpretability, accepting lower throughput.
Implement phased algorithm development with blinded cross-site data to avoid overfitting and test generalizability before deployment.
review: Core tension is automation speed versus accuracy; the options fail to require ongoing calibration across nights and sites to prevent drift in event adjudication.

name: Data governance and sharing scope under privacy constraints
consequences: Limiting sharing reduces privacy risk and simplifies compliance with ethics approvals. Broader sharing can accelerate external validation and collaboration, but requires robust governance and careful control over sensitive metadata and cross-border data handling.
options: Limit public data sharing to de-identified physiological datasets while implementing controlled access to robust metadata for qualified researchers.
Expand access with tiered permissions including synthetic datasets for broader collaboration while maintaining strict privacy controls and governance.
Partner with a dedicated data safety committee to audit sharing procedures and ensure ongoing regulatory compliance across jurisdictions.
review: Core tension is privacy versus openness; the options do not specify cross-border data transfer controls or agreement on data sovereignty in collaboration with non-local researchers.

name: Facility expansion timing and risk management
consequences: Expansion to 12 suites only after pilot validation protects program integrity and limits capital risk. A modular renovation plan offers scalability with demand but may extend the overall timeline and complicate staffing plans.
options: Proceed with physical expansion to 12 suites only after achieving pilot data quality benchmarks and acceptable false alarm rates to protect program integrity.
Adopt a modular renovation plan that scales with demand, allowing staged expansion without large upfront capital risk and enabling flexible staffing.
Pursue alternative simulation-based validation using high-fidelity virtual environments to reduce real-world expansion needs while preserving study design fidelity.
review: Core tension is expansion scale versus data quality and cost; the options ignore potential delays in renovations or regulatory approvals that could push timelines beyond plan.

name: Governance, funding strategy, and industry partnering
consequences: Diversified funding reduces reliance on a single pillar and can improve resilience. However, industry partnerships introduce potential conflicts of interest and publication constraints that require clear governance and policies.
options: Pursue phase-two grant proposals aligned with outcomes and independent replication to maximize scientific credibility and avoid over-reliance on industry funding.
Enter pharma partnership under strict publication and governance terms to align incentives while preserving researchers' autonomy and data control.
Establish a rotating external advisory board with milestone-driven budget triggers to ensure accountability, agility, and transparent resource allocation.
review: Core tension is diversification of funding versus independence; the options lack explicit conflict-of-interest policies and publication control safeguards.

name: Environmental protocol optimization for naturalistic observation
consequences: Optimizing the physical environment to support naturalistic behavior improves ecological validity of parasomnia observations and reduces participant reactivity. This shift may complicate standardized monitoring workflows and require additional safety controls and training to preserve data integrity across suites.
options: Redesign the sleep unit to maximize naturalistic bedroom ambience while maintaining safety thresholds, increasing ecological validity but potentially compromising monitoring granularity.
Implement modular privacy controls and predefined camera zones to protect participant dignity while preserving essential data streams for event triage.
Calibrate ambient conditions to minimize noise exposure and light while sustaining sensor power and reliability across long-stay periods.
review: Core tension arises between authentic sleep behavior and uniform data collection; the options miss how privacy constraints and off-axis camera placement can introduce blind spots in event detection.

name: Longitudinal participant engagement and retention protocol
consequences: Developing a structured, long-horizon engagement protocol can boost retention and data completeness across the multiweek stays. It may also elevate participant burden and influence consent dynamics, necessitating careful ethics oversight and monitoring of compensation levels.
options: Institute a staged consent and check-in protocol with regular partner briefings and interim feedback cycles to sustain engagement across multiweek stay.

Offer a flexible visit cadence paired with participant-centered compensation adjustments tied to completed monitoring milestones, reducing fatigue and dropout without compromising safety or data quality.
Create a participant advisory panel that reviews scheduling, privacy concerns, and comfort measures, translating feedback into concrete process changes that improve retention and trust.
review: Core tension centers on sustaining long-term participation without increasing coercive incentives or undue burden; the options miss that compensation structures could subtly pressure participants to remain.

name: Tiered data access and de-identification policy alignment
consequences: Implementing a tiered data access framework allocates different data detail levels based on risk and credentialing, balancing openness with participant privacy. This approach may slow external collaboration and require ongoing governance updates as new researchers join.
options: Institute a tiered data access framework that assigns different sufficiency levels of data detail to researchers based on risk, credentials, and project scope.
Standardize de-identification pipelines to strip identifiers before release, while maintaining linkable anonymized subsets for longitudinal analyses under secure agreements.
Negotiate data use agreements with external investigators that specify governance, audit rights, and publication embargo terms to protect participant privacy and consent.
review: Core tension rests in balancing openness of data with privacy protections; the options miss potential friction in external collaborations due to governance delays or inconsistent de-identification standards.

name: Staffing model resilience and shift pattern redesign
consequences: Redesigning staffing models to enhance resilience ensures round-the-clock safety and timely data annotation, improving data throughput and staffing efficiency. The approach may increase wage costs and require cross-training investments that stretch initial budgets.
options: Reorganize night-shift coverage to ensure two-person observer teams per suite, improving safety and data reliability at the cost of higher wage expenditures.
Implement cross-training across technicians and grant-funded contingency staff pools to adapt to unpredictable event loads without hampering data throughput.
Introduce a rotating on-call system with clear escalation paths for urgent events to maintain response times while preserving staff well-being.
review: Core tension lies in funding safety coverage versus overall budget; the options may overlook regulatory limits on staffing ratios or the risk of persistent overwork among staff.

name: Cross-site validation with Bonn clinical datasets
consequences: Establishing formal cross-site validation with Bonn clinical data enhances the robustness of event-triage methods and generalizability of findings. The collaboration may require complex governance, data transfer agreements, and alignment of scoring protocols.
options: Forge a formal data-sharing agreement with Bonn Epileptology to validate event triage signals against clinical PSG datasets, enhancing robustness and credibility.
Coordinate parallel pilots with external sleep centers to compare scoring consistency and generalize findings beyond single-site characteristics.
Publish joint methodological guidelines that harmonize scoring criteria and annotation protocols across sites to improve reproducibility.
review: Core tension is achieving consistent scoring and data formats across sites; the options may underestimate the effort required for protocol alignment and data transfer logistics.

name: Public dissemination and collaboration framework
consequences: Implementing a structured dissemination and collaboration framework expands stakeholder engagement, accelerates knowledge transfer, and supports grant applications. It may expose preliminary findings to misinterpretation and increases administrative overhead.
options: Establish a staged dissemination plan detailing open methods, de-identified datasets, and interim results to academic audiences while safeguarding participant privacy.
Create structured collaboration channels with patient advocacy groups and clinical partners to align on research priorities and accelerate translation.
Develop a governance charter for external collaborators outlining IP, authorship, and data stewardship to manage expectations and maintain ethical standards.
review: Core tension is balancing open dissemination with privacy safeguards and IP control; the options omit how legal terms may deter collaborators or create governance bottlenecks.

name: Adaptive expansion gating tied to pilot-phase data quality
consequences: Expanding only after pilot-phase data quality benchmarks are consistently met preserves safety and data integrity, but it can delay enrollment milestones and push publication timelines, potentially slowing overall study momentum.
options: Proceed with physical expansion only after achieving consistently met pilot-phase data quality benchmarks for two evaluation periods, prioritizing safety and data integrity over rapid capacity growth.

Implement staged expansion by adding one additional sleep suite per quarter contingent on meeting minimum annotation throughput and maintaining acceptable false alarm rates, ensuring rhythm of growth aligns with operational readiness.

Adopt a flexible, demand-driven expansion model that triggers modular renovations only when a critical mass of eligible participants is anticipated within a six-week window, balancing risk and opportunity.
review: Core tension: growth speed versus safety and data integrity; weakness: the options miss the financial runway and potential idle capacity during recruitment lags.

name: Nightly sensing cadence with escalation-night protocol
consequences: Shifting cadence to emphasize low-burden monitoring on most nights and reserve escalation PSG for flagged events reduces participant burden and data load while preserving critical diagnostic moments; downstream, it may require additional staffing on escalation nights and sophisticated synchronization across streams, potentially increasing operational complexity and cost.
options: Redesign nightly monitoring to emphasize low-burden modalities on all nights, reserving escalation PSG for events flagged by automated scoring within the same night to minimize participant disruption.
Introduce a randomized schedule where enhanced PSG nights occur unpredictably to reduce adaptation biases and better capture natural arousal patterns.
Implement a hybrid scheduling approach that aligns enhanced nights with periods historically associated with higher event yield, balancing data richness and participant comfort.
review: Core tension: data richness versus participant burden and staffing; weakness: options miss privacy and device reliability considerations on escalation nights.

name: Semi-automated event triage tool validation framework
consequences: Implementing a semi-automated triage tool guided by dual-human scoring reduces manual review workload and accelerates throughput while preserving diagnostic reliability; downstream, initial performance gaps may require ongoing human oversight and iterative annotation, delaying full autonomy and complicating data governance.
options: Develop and validate a semi-automated event triage tool using dual-human scorer gold standard, with iterative mismatch reviews to improve sensitivity and specificity over time.
Implement clinician-in-the-loop triage where algorithm-generated rankings are reviewed during on-site shifts, ensuring safety and accountability while gradually increasing automation.
Adopt a phased deployment that first processes a limited subset of events with strict performance metrics, then expands as reliability demonstrates workflow gains.
review: Core tension: automation benefits versus risk of misclassification; weakness: options miss that regulatory or ethical oversight may require ongoing manual review.

name: Participant retention through domestic-like environment calibration
consequences: Calibrating room ambience and routines to resemble a domestic setting can reduce stress and arousal, supporting higher retention and more naturalistic data; downstream, the approach increases facilities management costs, complicates standardization across participants, and may introduce behavioral bias toward home-like patterns.
options: Retain the domestic ambience by calibrating room acoustics and lighting to minimize arousal triggers, then measure effects on event frequency and participant satisfaction across cohorts.
Institute structured social and sleep-wake routines with partner involvement during visits to sustain engagement and reduce dropout risk.
Offer graduated compensation aligned with milestones and data contribution while maintaining strict ethical oversight and regulatory compliance.
review: Core tension: comfort and realism versus standardization; weakness: options overlook potential safety risks or privacy concerns in home-like settings.

name: Video data privacy risk mitigation in naturalistic setting
consequences: Implementing privacy controls preserves participant trust and regulatory compliance; downstream, video access limitations may reduce external validation opportunities and complicate triage tool development.
options: Enforce strict access controls and differential privacy on video metadata while storing video locally with limited retention, to reduce exposure and support compliance.
Redact identifiable features in real-time streams and apply automated anonymization before any off-site scoring, ensuring safety and privacy.
Prioritize de-identified physiological data sharing while constraining video distribution, establishing governance for data use agreements and oversight.
review: Core tension: data utility versus privacy risk; weakness: options miss that real-time redaction can degrade annotation quality.

name: Staffing mix resilience including cross-training for rapid incident response
consequences: Cross-training improves coverage robustness and safety across eight suites, reducing single points of failure; downstream, it raises training complexity, onboarding time, and potential dilution of specialized expertise.
options: Cross-train technicians to handle event scoring and monitoring tasks, enabling flexible staffing during peak nights and reducing overtime costs.
Create backfill shadow teams to guarantee continuous coverage in case of sick leave, ensuring safety without compromising data collection.
Establish on-call clinician support with a defined escalation path to the PI, balancing immediate safety needs with staffing burden and regulatory compliance.
review: Core tension: resilience versus cost and depth of expertise; weakness: options miss regulatory constraints on staffing ratios and required certifications.
